context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to do the City Case Study Spider Diagram for my Urban Studies climate course, and I’m a bit lost on how to start. I think it’s about mapping my city across the 5 pathways, but I’m not totally sure what kind of info belongs in each part.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—start with the assignment structure, not the city details yet.

First, tell me: what are the 5 pathways for urban transformation listed in your course materials? Once you name them, we can decide what evidence belongs under each one.

As a starting rule, each pathway section usually needs 2 things:
1. what the city is doing or planning
2. which actors, policies, or systems are involved

What city did you choose, and can you list the 5 pathways from your course page?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should keep this brief, stay focused on the specific assignment, and use guided discovery rather than explaining the whole task outright. The student is confused about how to start, so I will orient them to the assignment structure, ask a forward-moving question, and help them generate the categories of information they need without filling in the diagram for them. This follows the rules by using the Socratic method, avoiding direct completion of the assignment, and keeping the support bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I picked Mumbai. I’m blanking a little on the exact 5 pathway names though—I don’t want to guess wrong. Can you remind me of the pathway names, and then I’ll try sorting what kind of evidence would go under each one?
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t guess. Open the course page or notes and find the official wording—that matters here.

Look for the 5 pathway labels tied to urban transformation; they usually cover themes like technology/infrastructure, governance, economy, behavior, and planning/form. Once you paste the exact names here, we’ll sort Mumbai evidence under each one.

Can you pull up the official list and send it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for course-specific factual recall that is necessary to proceed. I can support memory without completing the assignment by prompting retrieval and directing them back to their materials. To stay Socratic and avoid giving too much, I should ask them to check the course page and report back, while giving a minimal cue about what the pathways generally cover so they can recognize them. This keeps the interaction on-task and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I found them, I think: urban form, infrastructure, governance, innovation, and lifestyle/consumption. I’m not 100% sure I copied the last two exactly, but those seem close. Is that basically the right set, and if so how do I tell the difference between, like, governance and innovation?
exchanges.tutor:
That looks close, but use the course’s exact wording in your submission.

To separate those two, ask:
- Governance: who makes decisions, sets rules, coordinates, or funds action?
- Innovation: what new tool, pilot, practice, or approach is being introduced?

Try this with one Mumbai example. Pick one climate action you’ve found, and tell me: is it mainly about decision-making, or mainly about a new solution being tried?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should affirm the student's progress without fully validating uncertain wording, keep them anchored to the assignment, and use comparison questions to help them distinguish categories on their own. The student asked about governance vs. innovation, so I will guide them to define each in terms of actors and mechanisms rather than supplying a finished classification scheme. This remains Socratic, concise, and directly useful for filling the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: Maybe Mumbai’s flood management stuff? I’m not sure if that’s governance or infrastructure. My first instinct is infrastructure because it sounds like drains and pumping, but if the city created a new flood plan or coordination system then maybe that shifts it toward governance. I’m kind of stuck on how “mainly” works when one example touches multiple pathways.
exchanges.tutor:
That overlap is normal. Don’t freeze on it.

Use this rule: classify by the primary mechanism, then note the overlap briefly.
- If the core action is physical systems being built or upgraded, think infrastructure.
- If the core action is rules, coordination, planning, or agency roles, think governance.

For your Mumbai flood example, what is the central action: building/upgrading something, or organizing/deciding something?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is productively grappling with category overlap, so I should validate that this is a normal feature of the assignment and guide them toward a workable decision rule rather than resolving the classification for them. I will use a simple heuristic—primary mechanism vs supporting mechanisms—and ask them to apply it to their Mumbai example. This keeps the response concise, Socratic, and focused on helping them make their own judgments without providing the filled-in spider diagram.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I’d put stormwater drainage upgrades mainly under infrastructure. If I mention a flood resilience plan or which municipal agencies coordinate it, that would be overlap with governance. That clicked a bit—do I need one example per pathway, or should I have a few under each?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that distinction is much better.

Unless your course page specifies a number, aim for enough evidence to show each pathway clearly. Usually that means at least one solid, specific example per pathway; add a second only if the first is weak or ambiguous.

What pathway do you want to tackle next for Mumbai: urban form, governance, innovation, or lifestyle/consumption?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress and now asks about scope. I should not invent assignment requirements if they are not explicitly stated, so I will guide them to use sufficiency and balance: enough evidence to show climate action across all five pathways without overloading. I will keep it concise, acknowledge the insight, and ask a next-step question that moves them into drafting their own diagram content.

exchanges.student: Let’s do urban form, because that one still feels fuzzy to me. I keep mixing it up with infrastructure, and I’m not sure if things like land use, density, or where development happens are the right kind of evidence.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—those are the right kinds of clues for urban form.

Try this distinction:
- Urban form = the spatial pattern of the city: land use, density, location of growth, street layout, where housing/jobs go.
- Infrastructure = the service systems: transport, drainage, water, energy, waste.

So ask yourself: is the policy shaping the city’s layout, or supplying/repairing a system?

Can you name one Mumbai example that changes where or how development happens?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants conceptual clarification between two pathways. I should help them distinguish urban form from infrastructure through guiding contrasts and examples at the level of categories, not by providing Mumbai-specific answers or ready-made wording. A Socratic prompt that asks what is being shaped—city layout versus service systems—will move them toward an independent classification rule.

exchanges.student: Maybe coastal zoning or limits on building in flood-prone areas? I think that changes where development can happen, so that sounds more like urban form than infrastructure. But I’m not sure if I’m being too vague yet.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right direction. The problem is not category now—it’s specificity.

Pressure-test it with 3 questions:
1. What is the actual policy or rule?
2. How does it change where building happens?
3. How does that relate to climate risk or emissions?

Answer those in notes for Mumbai. What specific zoning, coastal regulation, or land-use measure did you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a plausible urban form example but is worried about vagueness. I should validate the direction, then push them to make it more specific by identifying the policy/tool, the spatial effect, and the climate relevance. This keeps the response brief, Socratic, and focused on helping them generate stronger assignment content without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: I think it might be the Coastal Regulation Zone rules, but I need to check the exact Mumbai connection instead of just saying India has them. If I answer the 3 questions, I’d say: the rule limits or controls development near the coast, that affects where building is allowed, and it matters for flood/storm surge risk. Can you help me figure out whether that’s specific enough for the spider diagram or if I need a city-level Mumbai plan on top of it?
exchanges.tutor:
It’s almost specific enough, but only if you can tie it clearly to Mumbai rather than India in general.

Use this test:
- Is it clearly connected to Mumbai?
- Is the pathway obvious?
- Is the climate relevance obvious?

If one of those is weak, add a Mumbai-level plan, agency, or local application. What source do you have that shows this rule actually shapes development in Mumbai?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close to a workable entry and asks about adequacy. I should not decide the content for them, but I can provide a sufficiency test tied to the assignment and prompt them to verify Mumbai-specific relevance. This keeps the guidance bite-sized, Socratic, and prevents me from writing the submission for them.

exchanges.student: I don’t have that source yet, so I probably shouldn’t rely on it by itself. I think I need a Mumbai planning document or municipal source that shows how coastal or flood-risk land-use controls apply locally. If I can’t find that, I should switch to a clearer Mumbai urban-form example, right?
exchanges.tutor:
Correct. Don’t build your diagram on a shaky example.

Use the stronger rule: if you can’t verify the Mumbai link, replace it with a clearer city-specific urban form case.

Now choose: do you want to spend 10 minutes searching for a Mumbai planning source, or move on and identify a different urban-form example first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached the right conclusion independently. I should affirm that reasoning, keep momentum, and direct them to the next concrete step without supplying sources or examples outright. A short decision rule plus a focused question supports guided discovery and stays within the assignment.

exchanges.student: I’d move on first and come back, because I’m getting a little stuck on that one. Can we identify one clearer Mumbai urban-form example so I have something usable before I search more?
exchanges.tutor:
Good choice—don’t get trapped on one example.

Think Mumbai planning, not engineering: what about transit-oriented growth, redevelopment patterns, slum rehabilitation/resettlement, or development controls around risk areas?

Which of those have you actually seen mentioned for Mumbai? Pick one, and we’ll test whether it fits urban form.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on one sub-issue, so I should redirect to the bigger picture and keep momentum, as allowed by the rules. I must not provide a complete answer or write their diagram entry, but I can narrow the search space with leading prompts that help them generate a better Mumbai-specific urban-form example. I will stay concise, ask a forward-moving question, and avoid giving ready-made wording.
